title: Keema matar
subtitle: Minced lamb with peas and warm spices
excerpt: Minced lamb cooked with onion, ginger, garlic, and a blend of warming spices, finished with frozen peas that provide sweetness and colour against the dark, fragrant mince. A North Indian dish that cooks quickly (the mince needs no long braise) and produces a dry, intensely spiced result that works as a main, a naan filling, or a samosa stuffing.
sourceNotes: Keema matar (mince with peas) is a Punjabi-Mughal dish documented in Madhur Jaffrey's 'An Invitation to Indian Cooking' (1973) and widely reproduced in British-Indian cookbooks. Keema (mince) dishes are among the most practical Indian home cooking recipes, cooking in under 30 minutes.

content.text: Keema is a dry dish, the mince should be cooked until all the liquid has evaporated and the meat is frying in its own rendered fat. Achieving this requires patience: after the tomatoes are added and cook down, there will be a stage where moisture pools around the mince. This must be cooked off completely. The finished dish should be dry enough that the mince piles on a plate rather than spreading in liquid.

The peas go in right at the end. They need only 3 to 4 minutes to heat through and should remain bright green. If they cook longer, they dull and become mealy. The timing is: everything else is done, remove from heat, stir in frozen peas, return to low heat for 3 minutes.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a wide pan over medium-high heat. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 10 minutes until golden. Add the {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{turmeric}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{coriander-ground}}, and {{chilli-powder}} and stir for 1 minute. Add the {{lamb-mince}} and break up any clumps. Cook over high heat for 8 minutes, stirring frequently, until the mince is browned and any liquid has evaporated. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} and cook for 8 to 10 minutes, stirring, until reduced to a thick paste coating the mince. Stir in the {{garam-masala}} and salt. The mince should be dry.
Add the {{peas-frozen}} and stir to combine. Cook on low heat for 3 to 4 minutes until the peas are heated through and bright green. Scatter with {{coriander}} and serve with naan or rice.

items.fix: The mince must fry rather than braise. Increase the heat and cook uncovered, stirring, until the pan is dry and the mince is frying again
items.cause: Insufficient reduction or too much liquid added
items.symptom: Wet and saucy rather than dry

items.fix: Add peas in the final 3 to 4 minutes only. They should stay bright green. If they're greying, they have overcooked
Use a wooden spoon or spatula to break the mince into small pieces as it cooks. Large clumps don't brown evenly
After browning the mince, tilt the pan and spoon off any excess fat before adding the tomatoes
items.cause: Lamb mince too high in fat, excess not drained

content.text: Keema matar is one of the most useful dishes in Indian cooking. It uses cheap mince, cooks in 30 minutes, reheats well, and bends to many uses. Serve it with rice as a main, stuff it into naan, or use it as a samosa filling. On the British Indian menu it shows up as both a main dish and a smaller-portion filling. It is the base for keema naan, where the mince is stuffed into naan dough and cooked in the tandoor. One of the best bread dishes on any curry-house menu.
